column2: Suraj;2025;9;https://theseus.fi/handle/10024/901413;"This thesis presents a conversational chatbot system for discovering Finnish university theses. It is using “Retrieval-Augmented Generation” (RAG) technology. The project was developed during an internship at Nokia Garage Espoo. It integrated into Nokia Stand
column3: a collaborative platform showcasing Nokia and Metropolia University partnerships.

column1: The traditional keyword-based search on Theseus.fi
column2: Finland's national thesis repository
column3: requires exact questions and multiple attempts to find relevant content. This thesis addresses these limitations by implementing a RAG-based chatbot. The chatbot understands semantic meaning and enables natural language interaction
column4: interpreting what users want rather than simply matching keywords.

column1: The system has two main parts. The first part is a mobile app built with React Native. The second part is a backend built with Node.js that uses RAG technology. The backend collects thesis information from Theseus.fi. It then turns this data into vectors using a model called all-MiniLM-L6-v2. The system stores 45 Nokia-related theses in its memory. When users ask questions
column2: the system finds the right theses and creates answers using ready-made templates. This makes the system very fast. It gives answers in less than 0.5 seconds. All development
column3: implementation
column4: and testing were conducted in a local development environment. Testing showed that semantic search successfully retrieves relevant theses regardless of terminology differences
column5: demonstrating that RAG technology improves academic search experiences compared to traditional keyword matching.";YES;The thesis states it was developed during an internship at Nokia Garage Espoo and integrated into Nokia Stand.

column1: Net sales prediction : azure machine learning;Haaga-Helia UAS;Roy
column2: Sayak;2024;0;https://theseus.fi/handle/10024/860010;"This Master’s Thesis is a project-based thesis about Sales Prediction using Microsoft Azure Machine learning models for a telecommunication and technology company in Finland. The company has been following a semi-manual Sales forecasting procedure for many years. The purpose of the project was to evaluate the accuracy of the machine learning models in Microsoft Azure and their ability to forecast future sales. The work in the thesis is important because it provides a direction for the company to automate its sales forecast method with machine learning.

column1: In this thesis
column2: Azure machine learning algorithms were tested
column3: first with public dataset and then with company dataset to determine whether they provide similar results. The public dataset for Walmart sales was collected from Kaggle
column4: whereas the company historical sales was provided by the company after the data was scrambled. Both sample and training data consisted of actual historical sales for different departments and products. The data was pre-processed in SQL databases and was used as training datasets for Azure machine learning models.

column1: An Azure machine learning workspace was set up in company environment where training data was uploaded. Machine learning workspace was set up and multiple machine learning models (both regression and time series) were trained using Kaggle (Walmart sales) data. The machine learning models (after training) were fed with test dataset on which the model accuracy was measured using 'Normalized Root Mean Squared Error' metric. The best performing models were then isolated and were trained with the company specific data. At the end
column2: the top performing machine learning models were identified and listed. The results were compared with the existing studies using same Kaggle dataset and verified that if the top performing models are similar.

column1: Two algorithms
column2: XGBoost and Random Forest provided the best Normalized Root Mean squared Error metric and were listed as the top performing models in case of both Kaggle and company dataset and were marked as the candidates to be worked on
column3: in future for the company. The results of the experiments provide a baseline for generating predictions for company sales using Azure machine learning models and can be carried forward further in the desired direction.

column1: Abbreviations can be found at the end of the thesis. It is recommended that the reader has it available when reading the thesis
column2: which can be helpful in understanding all the terms used in the text.";NO;The thesis only refers to an unnamed telecommunication and technology company in Finland and does not mention Nokia or collaboration with Nokia.

column1: Time synchronized co-simulation for pre-silicon SoC validation;Tampere University;Rintakangas
column2: Jori;2025;16;https://trepo.tuni.fi/handle/10024/162408;"As the complexity of SoC (System-on-Chip) increases
column3: there is a need for new novel design and testing techniques. One way the chip manufacturers aim to speed up the development process is by using comprehensive pre-silicon SoC validation. Pre-silicon SoC validation
column4: also known as validation left-shifting is a process in which virtual platforms are used to test the integration of hardware and software components before the physical chip has been manufactured.

column1: Virtual platforms enable the creation of co-simulation environments
column2: in which simulators and emulators are used to concurrently model software and hardware components. An important as pect of a co-simulation environment is its ability to provide time synchronization between components so that the timing correctness and timing functionalities can be verified.

column1: This thesis is written in commission to Nokia Solutions and Networks Oy
column2: and it proposes a time synchronized co-simulation environment for pre-silicon SoC validation. The environment is built around Nokia’s in house developed TSS (Target SoC Simulator). TSS is a simulator for SoC simulation
column3: verification
column4: and validation and it provides mechanisms for connecting different types of models at different abstraction levels. TSS uses transaction objects to model register accesses between models. The environment includes QEMU (Quick Emulator) for processor and software modeling and a commercial logic simulator for RTL (Register Transfer Level) hardware modeling.

column1: The environment utilizes internal time synchronization support of TSS. Instruction counting mechanism of QEMU is utilized to derive an estimate of elapsed time of machine instructions executed by a guest processor. QEMU models a guest processor by translating guest code to host code in so called translation blocks. After each translation block execution
column2: elapsed time is calculated and passed to commercial simulator to advance its time accordingly. This procedure creates time synchronization between software and hardware models. Inter process communication methods such as named pipe and named semaphore are used to send timing information from QEMU to simulator.

column1: The proposed co-simulation environment is evaluated in different co-simulation setups to obtain speed and accuracy metrics. It is observed that the accuracy of the co-simulation depends on the type of software that is executed on guest processor modelled by QEMU. This is due to the fact that translation blocks created by QEMU vary in length. According to measurements
column2: the blocks tend to get longer when guest processor executes computationally intensive software. Longer translation blocks reduce the accuracy of the co-simulation
column3: but it improves the speed because of less frequent need of communication between QEMU and simulator.

column1: The communication delay between QEMU and commercial simulator is observed to be a major bottleneck that reduces the co-simulation performance. The large communication overhead stems from the usage of time consuming inter process communication methods. Another performance limiting factor is non-optimized transaction handling by RTL model
column2: that continuously polls for upcoming transactions. The future work regarding the proposed co-simulation environment should focus on improving the communication infrastructure between QEMU and simulator and optimizing transaction handling on the RTL side.";YES;The abstract states the thesis was written in commission to Nokia Solutions and Networks Oy and built around Nokia’s in-house developed TSS.

column1: The results show that the most common construction of topic headings did not have a verb at all. The average heading that included a verb had one verb and that verb was in present tense

column2: Aurora;2024;0;https://trepo.tuni.fi/handle/10024/155549;"The Tampere Deck Arena serves as an example of the large-scale growth oriented urban development projects that are increasingly encouraged by global neoliberal processes

column1: The objects of the analysis are tension points in a feedback compilation from the early planning phases of the arena project. By applying the method of Flyvbjerg et al.
column2: I identify problematic power relations apparent in the responses to the feedback and derive four place making strategies revealed in them.

column1: My discussion finds that the Deck Arena project was motivated by its perceived growth potential that the growth coalition behind its development – and in their interpretation the whole city – stood to gain benefits from and that the Deck Arena was legitimized with a problematic value-free perception of it. column8: yet it has largely ignored the processes that chance events generate. We address this issue by analyzing how chance events and political dynamics co-produce strategic change. We study the history of the Nokia Corporation between 1986 and 2015
column9: focusing on the event structures that led to the divestment of core businesses on two separate occasions. Our findings show how chance events and resulting political dynamics generate periods of collective indeterminacy where multiple
column10: competing strategic scenarios become apparent. column2: Jenni;2024;0;https://lutpub.lut.fi//handle/10024/168866;"The purpose of this bachelor’s thesis is to investigate whether the financial indicators of companies are related to their choice to report according to GRI 207 -standard. column2: turnover
column3: net income
column4: market capitalization
column5: year of incorporation
column6: number of employees
column7: ESG score and return on equity.

column1: Results indicate that only revenue is significantly associated with GRI reporting at a 10 % risk level. Companies with higher revenue are more likely to report according to the standard. column3: Ville;2025;0;https://lutpub.lut.fi//handle/10024/169293;"This bachelor’s thesis examines the ongoing CCUS (carbon capture utilization and stor-age) projects in Finland. News articles and information given on the websites of companies about the projects are used as material in this thesis. The theoretical part of the thesis explains what is meant by CCUS. column1: In the future CCUS technology is seen as an important tool in the fight against climate change and for reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide levels. In the climate targets set by the European Commission
column2: CCUS technology will play an important role in the EU's efforts to reach the carbon neutral limit by 2050 and to be carbon negative after 2050. Reducing emissions alone is not enough to reach this target which means that carbon dioxide must also be removed through capture.

column1: The results of the thesis show that the utilization of CCUS technology is still very scarce in Finland. Most of the projects are still in the planning stage and waiting for final investment decisions. However
column2: the situation could change significantly by the end of the decade if the planned projects are realised. In particular
column3: several investments are expected in the production of carbon dioxide-based fuels.";NO;The thesis page describes a LUT bachelor’s thesis on CCUS projects in Finland with no mention of Nokia collaboration

column2: Essi;2023;0;https://lutpub.lut.fi//handle/10024/165794;"Utilising traceability processes and systems has been recognised to have a positive contribution to sustainability.
